text: The surgeon clamps or ligates some of the vascular supply of the pouch to obtain sufficient length for the anastomosis during the elongation procedure.
The surgeon uses a vascular clamp to occlude the vascular supply, visibly restricting blood flow to the targeted area.
The screen displays three visual modes: normal light, infrared light, and enhanced reality, each providing different visual perspectives of the surgical field.
Under black and white light, the surgeon demonstrates that the vascular supply to the pouch remains intact following the elongation procedure.
The surgeon divides the vessels using surgical instruments, following the clamping of the vascular supply.
The surgeon positions the pouch at the bottom end in preparation for the anastomosis, ensuring it is safely aligned for the procedure.
The surgeon checks the anastomosis from the outside using an intra-abdominal view, with the joint visible at the top end of the surgical field.
The surgeon injects endocyanin, causing the vessels and microvessels to fluoresce brightly under infrared light, clearly visible up to the level of the anastomosis.
The surgeon performs an endoanal view of the G-pouch, inspecting the interior for any signs of bleeding and assessing the appearance of the pouch lining.
The surgeon assesses the vascularization of the surgical site before proceeding with the creation of an ileostomy.
The surgeon inspects the stable line of the anastomosis, clearly visible in the intra-abdominal view, and identifies any bleeding sites for precise localization and stitching.
